questions.question: Define the Interval Scheduling Problem and explain the greedy approach used to solve it.
keyPoints: Interval Scheduling selects maximum non-overlapping intervals from a set
Greedy approach: Sort intervals by finish time in ascending order
Select first interval, then repeatedly select next compatible interval (one that starts after last selected finishes)
Time complexity O(n log n) due to sorting

questions.question: Explain the Fractional Knapsack Problem. How does the greedy algorithm solve it?
keyPoints: Given n items with values and weights, maximize value with knapsack capacity W
Can take fractions of items (unlike 0/1 knapsack)
Greedy: Sort by value/weight ratio in descending order
Take items fully until an item can't fit completely, then take fraction of it
Time complexity O(n log n)

questions.question: Compare and contrast 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack problems.
keyPoints: 0/1: take whole item or nothing; Fractional: can take any fraction
0/1 requires dynamic programming; Fractional has greedy solution
Greedy fails for 0/1 because taking high ratio item may exclude better combination
Both have optimal substructure but only Fractional has greedy choice property

questions.question: What is the Greedy Choice Property? How does it apply to Interval Scheduling?
keyPoints: Greedy Choice Property: local optimal choice leads to global optimum
For Interval Scheduling: choosing earliest finishing interval never harms optimality
Proof via exchange argument: replace first interval of optimal solution with earliest finisher
This leaves maximum room for remaining intervals

questions.question: Write pseudocode for the Fractional Knapsack algorithm and analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Input: n items with (value, weight), knapsack capacity W
Step 1: Calculate value/weight ratio for each item
Step 2: Sort items in descending order by ratio
Step 3: For each item, if it fits completely, take it; else take fraction
Time Complexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
Space Complexity: O(1) besides input storage

questions.question: Explain the exchange argument proof technique used for Interval Scheduling correctness.
keyPoints: Assume an optimal solution that doesn't start with earliest finishing interval
Find first interval in optimal solution that could be swapped
Exchange it with the earliest finishing interval
The exchange doesn't reduce number of intervals (earliest finisher finishes no later)
By repeatedly applying, we transform any optimal solution to greedy solution

questions.question: Solve the following Interval Scheduling instance: Intervals given as (start, finish): (1,4), (2,3), (3,6), (4,5), (5,7), (6,8). Show step-by-step which intervals are selected by the greedy algorithm and justify why this is optimal.
keyPoints: Sort intervals by finish time: (2,3), (1,4), (4,5), (3,6), (5,7), (6,8)
Initialize: select (2,3), last_finish = 3
Check (1,4): start 1 < 3, skip
Check (4,5): start 4 >= 3, select, last_finish = 5
Check (3,6): start 3 < 5, skip
Check (5,7): start 5 >= 5, select, last_finish = 7
Check (6,8): start 6 < 7, skip
Selected intervals: (2,3), (4,5), (5,7) - Total 3 intervals
Optimality: Greedy with earliest finish time is always optimal
Proof by exchange argument

questions.question: Consider a Fractional Knapsack with capacity 50 and the following items: Item A: value=60, weight=10; Item B: value=100, weight=20; Item C: value=120, weight=30. Using the greedy algorithm, determine the optimal solution. Show all calculations and explain why this solution is optimal.
keyPoints: Sort by ratio descending: A(6), B(5), C(4)
Initialize: capacity=50, total_value=0
Take Item A (weight 10): capacity remaining=40, value=60
Take Item B (weight 20): capacity remaining=20, value=160
Take Item C: only 20 of 30 fits, fraction=20/30, value added=120*(20/30)=80
Total value = 60+100+80 = 240
Wait - recalculate: A(10) + B(20) + C(20/30*30)=10+20+20=50 capacity used
Optimality: Greedy choice property holds because any other combination gives less or equal value

questions.question: Prove formally that the greedy algorithm for Interval Scheduling produces an optimal solution. Use the exchange argument.
keyPoints: Let intervals be sorted by finish time: I1, I2, ..., In
Let greedy algorithm select G = {g1, g2, ..., gk}
Let O = {o1, o2, ..., om} be optimal solution with maximum intervals
Base case: g1 has earliest finish time among all intervals
If o1 = g1, then remaining problem is same structure - use induction
If o1 ≠ g1: Since g1 finishes earliest, f(g1) ≤ f(o1)
Replace o1 with g1 - this doesn't reduce number of intervals (exchange)
By repeatedly applying exchange, transform O to include all of G
Therefore |G| = |O|, greedy is optimal

questions.question: Design an algorithm for the Weighted Interval Scheduling problem where each interval has a weight (value). How does it differ from the unweighted version? Explain with example.
keyPoints: Unlike unweighted, weighted version requires dynamic programming
Sort by finish time, then compute DP[i] = max(DP[i-1], weight[i] + DP[p(i)])
p(i) = largest index j < i such that interval j doesn't conflict with i
DP approach necessary because greedy fails for weighted version
Example: intervals with values: (1,4,50), (2,3,100), (3,5,80), (4,6,70)
Greedy by finish time might select (2,3) + (4,6) = 170, but (1,4)+(3,5)+(4,6) could be better
Time complexity: O(n log n) with binary search for p(i), or O(n²) naive
Space: O(n) for DP table
